Symmetric Top (Rigid Body)

This demo simulates a symmetric top — a rigid body with two equal principal moments of inertia \(\lambda_1 = \lambda_2\) and a distinct third moment \(\lambda_3\) about its symmetry axis. The body is free to rotate about a fixed pivot at the origin under the influence of gravity.

Euler Angles

The orientation of the body is described by three Euler angles (ZYZ convention). These are purely geometric — they specify how the body frame is rotated relative to the space frame, regardless of the dynamics:

  • \(\phi\) — rotation about the space-fixed \(z\)-axis. It specifies the azimuthal direction in which the symmetry axis points.
  • \(\theta\) — the tilt angle between the body symmetry axis and the vertical \(z\)-axis. \(\theta = 0\) means the body axis is upright.
  • \(\psi\) — rotation of the body about its own symmetry axis.

Toggle Show Euler angles in the control panel to see the arcs for each angle overlaid on the scene.

Equations of Motion

Because \(\phi\) and \(\psi\) are cyclic coordinates (the Lagrangian doesn't depend on them directly), their conjugate momenta are conserved:

\[ L_z = \lambda_1\,\dot\phi\,\sin^2\!\theta + \lambda_3\bigl(\dot\psi + \dot\phi\cos\theta\bigr)\cos\theta \] \[ L_3 = \lambda_3\bigl(\dot\psi + \dot\phi\cos\theta\bigr) \]

\(L_z\) is the component of angular momentum along the space-fixed \(z\)-axis, and \(L_3\) is the component along the body symmetry axis. The remaining equation for \(\theta\) is:

\[ \lambda_1\,\ddot\theta = \lambda_1\,\dot\phi^2\sin\theta\cos\theta - L_3\,\dot\phi\,\sin\theta + Mg R\,\sin\theta \]

where \(MgR\) combines the mass, gravitational acceleration, and the distance from the pivot to the center of mass.

Precession and Nutation

The dynamics of the symmetric top give rise to two characteristic motions:

  • Precession — the symmetry axis sweeps around the vertical. This appears as a steady change in \(\phi\), driven by the gravitational torque.
  • Nutation — the tilt angle \(\theta\) oscillates up and down as the top precesses. The range of this oscillation depends on the initial conditions.

In general, both motions happen simultaneously. However, for special initial conditions, the top can undergo steady precession — \(\theta\) remains exactly constant while \(\phi\) increases at a uniform rate. This requires \(\dot\theta = 0\) and \(\ddot\theta = 0\), which gives the condition:

\[ \lambda_1\cos\theta\;\dot\phi^2 - L_3\,\dot\phi + MgR = 0 \]

The Set steady precession button computes the \(\dot\phi\) that satisfies this for the current \(\theta\) and \(\dot\psi\).

Controls

Adjust the physical parameters and initial angular velocities in the right-hand panel, then press Run to start the simulation. Drag to orbit the camera, scroll to zoom.
